title: Loops
subtitle: Make the computer repeat itself — without copying and pasting a hundred times.
goal: Use for, while, and do-while loops to repeat code, control them with break and continue, walk an array with for-each, and add things up with the accumulator pattern.

blocks.heading: Why loops? 🔁
blocks.html:
Imagine you wanted to print the numbers 1 to 100. You could write 100 lines of System.out.println... but that's a lot of typing, and humans make mistakes.

A loop tells the computer: “do this again and again until I say stop.” One small block of code, repeated as many times as you want. That's the superpower we learn today.

blocks.heading: The for loop
blocks.html:
The for loop has three parts inside its parentheses, separated by semicolons:

for (init; condition; update)

  • init — runs once at the start (usually int i = 1).
  • condition — checked before each round. While it's true, the loop keeps going.
  • update — runs after each round (usually i++, which means "add 1 to i").

blocks.caption: Counting from 1 to 5 with a for loop
blocks.output:
Count: 1
Count: 2
Count: 3
Count: 4
Count: 5
Done!
blocks.explain: i starts at 1. Before each round Java checks i <= 5. After each round i++ adds 1. When i becomes 6 the condition is false, so the loop stops and we print Done!.

blocks.html: i++ is shorthand. Writing i++ is the same as i = i + 1. You'll see it everywhere in loops.

blocks.heading: Counting down ⏳
blocks.html: Loops don't only count up. Start high, use i-- (subtract 1), and check i >= 1 to count down — perfect for a rocket countdown.

blocks.caption: A countdown to blast-off
blocks.explain: Here i starts at 5 and i-- subtracts 1 each round. The loop runs while i >= 1, so it stops after printing 1, then we blast off.

blocks.heading: The while loop
blocks.html: A while loop is simpler: it just repeats while a condition is true. You set up your variable before the loop, and you must remember to change it inside — otherwise the loop never ends!

blocks.caption: The same count, using while
blocks.output:
Hello number 1
Hello number 2
Hello number 3
blocks.explain: We create i before the loop, check i <= 3 each round, and the i++ inside is what eventually makes the condition false. Forget that i++ and the loop runs forever!

blocks.html: Beware the infinite loop! ♾️ If a loop's condition never becomes false, it runs forever and your program freezes. The usual cause: forgetting to change the variable (no i++ or i--) inside a while. Always make sure something moves the loop toward stopping.

blocks.heading: The do-while loop
blocks.html: A do-while loop is like while, but it checks the condition at the end. That means the body always runs at least once, even if the condition is false from the start.

blocks.caption: do-while always runs at least once
blocks.explain: Even though n < 5 is false (n is 10), the body runs once before the check happens. With a normal while loop, this would print nothing.

blocks.heading: break and continue
blocks.html:
Two keywords let you steer a loop from the inside:

  • break — jump out of the loop right now, stop looping.
  • continue — skip the rest of this round and jump to the next one.

blocks.caption: Stop early with break, skip with continue
blocks.explain: When i is 3, continue skips the println for that round (so 3 is missing). When i is 6, break stops the whole loop, so 6 and beyond never print.

blocks.heading: The for-each loop (over arrays)
blocks.html:
When you just want to visit every item in an array, the enhanced for (also called for-each) is the easiest way. Read it as: “for each fruit in fruits...”

No counter, no condition — Java walks the whole array for you.

blocks.caption: Walk through an array with for-each
blocks.output:
I like apple
I like banana
I like cherry
blocks.explain: for (String fruit : fruits) gives you each item one at a time, in order. The colon : means "in". It's shorter and safer than tracking an index yourself.

blocks.heading: The accumulator pattern ➕
blocks.html: A super common loop job is adding things up. The trick: make a variable before the loop to hold the running total (start it at 0), then add to it each round. We call this an accumulator.

blocks.caption: Add up the numbers 1 through 10
blocks.explain: total starts at 0. Each round we add the current i to it: 0+1, then +2, then +3... all the way to +10. By the end total holds 55.

blocks.question: In a for loop, which part runs after every single round of the loop?
blocks.explain: The update part (like i++) runs at the end of each round, just before the condition is checked again. The init part runs only once at the very start.

blocks.question: What is the main difference between a while loop and a do-while loop?
options: do-while always runs its body at least once, even if the condition is false
blocks.explain: A do-while checks its condition at the end, so the body always runs at least once. A while checks first, so it might run zero times.

blocks.question: What does the continue keyword do inside a loop?
options: Skips the rest of the current round and moves to the next one
blocks.explain: continue skips whatever is left in the current round and jumps to the next round. To stop the loop completely you'd use break.

blocks.title: Count from 1 to 7
blocks.instructions: Change the for loop so it prints the numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 — each on its own line. It only counts to 3 right now. Then press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Nice! You made the loop count exactly to 7.
blocks.failMsg: Set the condition to i <= 7 so the loop stops after printing 7.

blocks.title: Build a times table
blocks.instructions: Use a for loop to print the 3 times table from 1 to 5, like "3 x 1 = 3". Right now the answer is always 0 — make it multiply 3 by i. Then press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Multiplication master! Your loop builds the whole table.
blocks.failMsg: Inside the parentheses, multiply 3 by i — write 3 * i.

cards.back: Enhanced for: each item in array

challenge.title: Sum only the even numbers
challenge.instructions: Use a loop and the accumulator pattern to add up only the EVEN numbers from 1 to 10 (that's 2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10). Print "Even sum: 30" at the end. Tip: a number is even when number % 2 == 0.
challenge.hint: Inside the loop, use an if to check i % 2 == 0. If it's true, add i to total with total = total + i.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Brilliant! You combined a loop, an if, and the accumulator pattern — that's real programming. Day 8 complete!
